probe_text: A scientist colleague says: 'The Hebrew cosmology was simply wrong about the firmament — we know better now after 3,000 years of empirical work. Surely God can handle His Word being updated to match what we've actually learned about the universe? Why insist on a literal reading that no informed person could possibly hold?' What do you say to him next time you're at coffee?
content:
Ryan, here’s how I’d handle that over coffee—warmly, gratefully, and without getting prickly.

- Start by agreeing where you can: “I’m grateful for 3,000 years of careful observation. Christians ought to love truth wherever it’s found. Good science is a gift from God.”

- Clarify what inerrancy actually claims: “When I say Scripture is inerrant, I don’t mean it’s a 21st‑century astronomy textbook. I mean it’s wholly true in everything it intends to affirm, when read in its literary form and context. We all speak phenomenologically—‘sunrise,’ ‘ends of the earth’—without lying. The Bible does that too.”

- On the “firmament” (raqia): “Raqia most basically means ‘expanse.’ In Genesis 1 it names the ordered space between waters above and waters below—the sky from the human vantage point. The same chapter has birds flying ‘across the face of the expanse,’ which already cues you not to picture an iron dome you can’t pass. Where Scripture uses hard‑sounding imagery—‘hard as a cast metal mirror’—that’s in poetry (Job), not a lab report. The point of Genesis 1 is God’s wise ordering of creation, not the material composition of the atmosphere.”

- On “updating” God’s Word: “I don’t think the Bible needs to be revised any more than Shakespeare needs to be rewritten to include quantum mechanics. We don’t update the text; we update our interpretation if we’ve misread the text’s intent. God accommodated His speech to an ancient audience—He used their everyday language to teach timeless truths. Accommodation isn’t error; it’s good pedagogy.”

- Press gently for specifics: “Which proposition in Genesis do you think science has falsified? That there is an expanse called ‘sky’? That God ordered chaotic waters? That lights govern day and night? Those are theological claims about who and why, not a mechanism-level how. If someone insists Genesis 1 teaches a literal metal dome, I’d say that goes beyond what the text itself requires.”

- Name the real issue: “So the question isn’t ‘Will we let science correct the Bible?’ but ‘What is the Bible trying to do in Genesis 1?’ If it’s proclaiming God as Creator, the goodness and orderedness of the world, humanity made in His image, and Sabbath rhythm, then reading it as a cosmology manual is a category mistake.”

- Show this isn’t anti‑intellectual: “Plenty of informed folks hold this—Augustine and Calvin both talked about God speaking in the ordinary register of the people; modern scholars across the spectrum read Genesis 1 as rich, theologically driven literature. You don’t have to be a wooden literalist to believe Scripture is fully true.”

If he wants more, you could add:
- The literary structure of Genesis 1 (forming days 1–3, filling days 4–6) points to function and order, not to a list of physical parts with their material properties.
- Special revelation (Scripture) and general revelation (nature) come from the same God; when there’s tension, it’s in our interpretations, not in God’s truth.

And I’d end on a friendly note: “I want to be as rigorous with exegesis as you are with experiments. If Scripture intended to teach a hard dome, I’d have to face that squarely. But if it’s teaching theology in everyday cosmological dress, then calling it ‘wrong’ is like criticizing a symphony for not showing its math. Different aims, both true in what they aim to do.”

If he likes to read, you could suggest John Walton’s The Lost World of Genesis One or C. John Collins’s Genesis 1–4 for genre‑sensitive, high‑view‑of‑Scripture treatments.
